verses.text: God bids Jeremiah write in a book, for 'the days come... that I will bring again the captivity of my people.' Restoration is promised in writing. God records His mercies so His people may hold to them.

God bids Jeremiah write in a book, for 'the days come... that I will bring again the captivity of my people.' Restoration is promised in writing. God records His mercies so His people may hold to them.

God bids Jeremiah write in a book, for 'the days come... that I will bring again the captivity of my people.' Restoration is promised in writing. God records His mercies so His people may hold to them.
A time of trembling comes, 'the time of Jacob's trouble; but he shall be saved out of it.' Distress will be great. Yet through the deepest trouble God brings His people safely out.
A time of trembling comes, 'the time of Jacob's trouble; but he shall be saved out of it.' Distress will be great. Yet through the deepest trouble God brings His people safely out.
A time of trembling comes, 'the time of Jacob's trouble; but he shall be saved out of it.' Distress will be great. Yet through the deepest trouble God brings His people safely out.
A time of trembling comes, 'the time of Jacob's trouble; but he shall be saved out of it.' Distress will be great. Yet through the deepest trouble God brings His people safely out.
He will break the foreign yoke, and they shall serve 'the LORD their God, and David their king.' Freedom is restored. This points to Christ, the Son of David, under whose reign His people serve.
He will break the foreign yoke, and they shall serve 'the LORD their God, and David their king.' Freedom is restored. This points to Christ, the Son of David, under whose reign His people serve.

'Fear not, O my servant Jacob'; God will save him from afar, making 'a full end of all nations,' but not of him, only correcting in measure. He distinguishes His own. God ends the wicked but chastens His people gently.

'Fear not, O my servant Jacob'; God will save him from afar, making 'a full end of all nations,' but not of him, only correcting in measure. He distinguishes His own. God ends the wicked but chastens His people gently.
Their bruise seems incurable, their lovers gone, for the greatness of their sin; why cry over a self-inflicted wound? Judgment is just. They suffer the harvest of their own iniquity.
Their bruise seems incurable, their lovers gone, for the greatness of their sin; why cry over a self-inflicted wound? Judgment is just. They suffer the harvest of their own iniquity.
Their bruise seems incurable, their lovers gone, for the greatness of their sin; why cry over a self-inflicted wound? Judgment is just. They suffer the harvest of their own iniquity.
Their bruise seems incurable, their lovers gone, for the greatness of their sin; why cry over a self-inflicted wound? Judgment is just. They suffer the harvest of their own iniquity.
Yet 'they that devour thee shall be devoured,' and God will 'restore health unto thee, and... heal thy wounds.' Mercy follows. The God who wounded for sin will Himself heal the penitent.
Yet 'they that devour thee shall be devoured,' and God will 'restore health unto thee, and... heal thy wounds.' Mercy follows. The God who wounded for sin will Himself heal the penitent.

He will rebuild the tents of Jacob, raise up their own leaders, and seal the bond: 'ye shall be my people, and I will be your God.' The covenant is renewed. Restoration ends in the sweetest of all promises.

He will rebuild the tents of Jacob, raise up their own leaders, and seal the bond: 'ye shall be my people, and I will be your God.' The covenant is renewed. Restoration ends in the sweetest of all promises.

The whirlwind of the LORD's anger goes forth against the wicked; 'in the latter days ye shall consider it.' Judgment and mercy stand together. What seems dark now will be understood in God's appointed time.
